content:
# Acts Chapter 26 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 257-258 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_026_page_001.jpg, ACT_026_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And Agrippa said to Paul, "You are permitted to speak on your own behalf." Then Paul, having stretched out his hand, began to make his defense:

**2** "Concerning all the things of which I am accused by the Jews, King Agrippa, I consider myself blessed that I am about to make my defense before you today, **3** especially because you are an expert in all the customs and controversies of the Jews. Therefore I beg you to hear me patiently.

**4** "My manner of life from my youth, which from the beginning was spent among my own nation and in Jerusalem, all the Jews know, **5** having known me from the first — if they are willing to testify — that according to the strictest sect of our religion I lived as a Pharisee. **6** And now I stand being judged for the hope of the promise made by God to our fathers, **7** to which our twelve tribes, earnestly serving God night and day, hope to attain. Concerning this hope I am accused by the Jews, O king! **8** Why is it judged incredible among you if God raises the dead?

**9** "I myself indeed thought it necessary to do many things in opposition to the name of Jesus the Nazarene, **10** which I also did in Jerusalem. And many of the saints I shut up in prisons, having received authority from the chief priests; and when they were being put to death, I cast my vote against them. **11** And throughout all the synagogues, punishing them often, I was compelling them to blaspheme; and being exceedingly enraged against them, I was persecuting them even to foreign cities.

**12** "While so engaged, as I was journeying to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests, **13** at midday along the road, O king, I saw a light from heaven, surpassing the brightness of the sun, shining around me and those journeying with me. **14** And when we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language, 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads.' **15** And I said, 'Who are you, Lord?' And the Lord said, 'I am Jesus whom you are persecuting. **16** But rise and stand upon your feet; for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you as a servant and a witness both of the things in which you have seen me and of the things in which I will appear to you, **17** delivering you from the people and from the Gentiles, to whom I am sending you, **18** to open their eyes, that they may turn from darkness to light and from the authority of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and an inheritance among those who have been sanctified by faith in me.'

**19** "Therefore, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision, **20** but declared first to those in Damascus and then in Jerusalem, and throughout all the region of Judea, and to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, doing works worthy of repentance. **21** On account of these things the Jews, having seized me in the temple, were attempting to kill me. **22** Having therefore obtained the help that is from God, I stand to this day, testifying both to small and great, saying nothing beyond what both the prophets and Moses said would come to pass — **23** that the Christ must suffer, and that being the first of the resurrection from the dead, he would proclaim light both to the people and to the Gentiles."

**24** And as he was saying these things in his defense, Festus said with a loud voice, "You are mad, Paul! Your great learning is driving you to madness!" **25** But Paul said, "I am not mad, most excellent Festus, but I am speaking words of truth and soundness of mind. **26** For the king knows about these things, to whom also I speak with boldness. For I am persuaded that none of these things escapes his notice, for this has not been done in a corner. **27** King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe." **28** And Agrippa said to Paul, "In a short time you are persuading me to become a Christian!" **29** And Paul said, "I would pray to God, whether in a short time or in a long time, that not only you but also all who hear me today might become such as I am — except for these chains."

**30** And the king rose up, and the governor, and Bernice, and those sitting with them. **31** And having withdrawn, they were speaking to one another, saying, "This man is doing nothing worthy of death or of chains." **32** And Agrippa said to Festus, "This man could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **v.1 — "stretched out his hand" (εκτεινας την χειρα):** A formal rhetorical gesture in the Greco-Roman world. Paul adopts the posture of an orator before the royal audience. This is preserved literally rather than simplified, as it conveys the forensic and ceremonial setting.

2. **v.5 — "sect" (αιρεσιν):** The Greek αιρεσις here carries its neutral sense of "party" or "school of thought" — not the later pejorative meaning of "heresy." Rendered "sect" as the most faithful English equivalent in this context, consistent with Acts 24:5 and 24:14.

3. **v.7 — "twelve tribes" (δωδεκαφυλον):** Sinaiticus reads the single compound word δωδεκαφυλον ("the twelve-tribed [nation]"), a term unique to Acts in the New Testament. It refers to Israel conceived as a unified people, not merely the tribes of Judah and Benjamin. Rendered "twelve tribes" for clarity while preserving the corporate scope.

4. **v.10 — "I cast my vote" (κατηνεγκα ψηφον):** Literally "I brought down a pebble," referring to the ancient practice of voting with stones. This has led some to speculate that Paul was a member of the Sanhedrin. Rendered "I cast my vote" to preserve the juridical weight of the phrase.

5. **v.14 — "in the Hebrew language" (τη Εβραιδι διαλεκτω):** This likely refers to Aramaic, the common Semitic language of first-century Palestine, though it could mean Hebrew proper. Retained as "the Hebrew language" following the manuscript's own terminology. The proverb about kicking against goads (κεντρα) is a Greek expression known from Euripides and Aeschylus, suggesting a culturally resonant rendering of Christ's words.

6. **v.14 — "goads" (κεντρα):** The pointed sticks used to drive oxen. This proverb appears in the Damascus Road account only here in Acts 26, not in the earlier tellings (Acts 9, 22). The Sinaiticus text includes this detail. The metaphor speaks of futile resistance against divine purpose.

7. **v.18 — "the authority of Satan" (της εξουσιας του σατανα):** The Greek εξουσια is rendered "authority" rather than "power" (δυναμις) to preserve the distinction between dominion/jurisdiction and raw power. Satan's rule is presented as a competing jurisdiction from which people are transferred to God's.

8. **v.23 — "first of the resurrection" (πρωτος εξ αναστασεως νεκρων):** Sinaiticus reads πρωτος ("first") rather than a variant. This affirms Christ as the first to rise from the dead in the eschatological sense — the firstfruits of the general resurrection. The phrase is rendered formally to preserve the theological precision.

9. **v.24 — "soundness of mind" (σωφροσυνης):** The Greek σωφροσυνη denotes self-control, prudence, and mental clarity — a cardinal virtue in Greek philosophical thought. Rendered "soundness of mind" rather than "sobriety" or "reason" to capture both the intellectual and moral dimensions Paul claims for his testimony.

10. **v.28 — "In a short time" (εν ολιγω):** Agrippa's famous reply. The phrase εν ολιγω is ambiguous — it can mean "in a short time," "with little effort," or "with few words." Some traditions render this as "almost" (cf. KJV "Almost thou persuadest me"). The Sinaiticus text supports the temporal reading, and Paul's reply in v.29 (εν ολιγω και εν μεγαλω, "in a short time or in a long time") confirms the temporal contrast. This is preserved rather than the traditional but interpretive "almost."

11. **v.32 — "appealed to Caesar" (επικεκλητο Καισαρα):** The pluperfect form of επικαλεομαι marks the legal appeal as an irrevocable past action. Agrippa's statement confirms Paul's innocence while acknowledging that the legal machinery of Rome, once invoked, cannot be reversed. This forms the theological-political climax of Paul's juridical narrative in Acts.

12. **Textual note — Sinaiticus readings:** Sinaiticus contains characteristic orthographic features throughout this passage, including itacistic vowel interchanges. In v.20 Sinaiticus reads "throughout all the region of Judea" with slight variation from other manuscript traditions. These scribal features do not alter the sense of the text and are translated according to their intended meaning.

— LOGOS
